GB1-W05-A1 - Block Punches from Closed G.body:
## Concept

# Block Punches from Closed Guard + Distance Mgmt + Control Arms + Armbar

**Position:** Closed Guard  
**Category:** Submission (Punch Defense)  
**Curriculum:** GB1 Week 5 A1  
**Confidence:** 4/5

A practical self-defense and sport technique: when the opponent is postured in your closed guard and begins striking, you use frames and distance management to control their arms, break their posture, and finish with an armbar (or transition to triangle/kimura).

GB affiliate instruction for the connected closed guard attack system (Scottsdale Week 9/13 series) provides the core hip elevation, posting hip base, arm control, and angle creation mechanics that make this high-percentage. Specific long-form GB affiliate video for the exact "Week 5 block punches" title was limited in public search; we therefore leverage the closely related GB Scottsdale closed guard attack content (already deeply reviewed for triangle, kimura, pendulum) for the shared posture/hip/arm principles.

## Setup

Opponent in your closed guard, sitting up and threatening or throwing punches.

- Strong frames (forearms or shins) to jam strikes at the source.
- One or both arms become extended or committed as they strike or post.
- Hips active; collar or sleeve grip ready for control.

Wikilink connections:
- The punch block, arm control, and posture break lead directly into the detailed armbar finish in [[GB1-W09-A3 - Armbar from Guard]] (high leg placement / knee line concept from GB Scottsdale).
- Same distance management and hip elevation as [[GB1-W05-B1 - Block Punches from Closed Guard + Distance Mgmt + Up Kick + Technical Lift]] and the Week 13 clinch + triangle variant we just enriched with GB Scottsdale mechanics.
- Feeds the triangle retention cluster ([[GB1-W09-B3 - Triangle from Closed Guard]], Roy Dean stack counter).

## Execution

### Frame and Jam the Punches
1. Create a barrier with forearms, shins, or a strong collar tie. Jam at the elbows/shoulders — do not reach for the strikes.
2. As they strike or post, trap one arm (overhook, underhook, sleeve, or wrist control). Pull it across your centerline.

### Break Posture + Hip Elevation (GB-Reviewed Mechanics)
1. Use the trapped arm + strong knee drive to pull the opponent forward while elevating your hips (the same hip elevation + posting hip base principle deeply reviewed from GB Scottsdale Week 9 Pendulum and Week 13 triangle/kimura content).
2. Keep weight on the posting hip/knee for stability while the active hip creates the angle.

### Isolate Arm and Finish (Armbar or Triangle Transition)
1. With one arm isolated and posture broken, bring the leg on the trapped-arm side high across their shoulder/back (knee line high — the GB Scottsdale "cutting angle" cue).
2. Feed the arm across and extend for the armbar, or transition to triangle if they defend by posture or stack (use the hip escape to re-create angle under forward pressure — exact GB Scottsdale retention mechanic).
3. Squeeze with the legs and walk shoulders toward their far hip.

### Hand Placement & References
- **Punch jam to arm control**: Forearm/shin frames first, then sleeve or wrist grip high on the committed arm. Same control family as the wrist isolation in the GB Scottsdale Kimura review and the collar feed in X-Collar/Triangle.
- **High leg for finish (knee line)**: When entering the armbar, the leg must cut high on the shoulder/upper back (not low). Direct application of the GB Scottsdale triangle "knee line over shoulder" cue that creates the tight angle even against forward drive.
- **Hip elevation + posting base**: The closed guard does the heavy lifting. Weight shifts to the posting hip during elevation and retention — the core stability detail reviewed across GB Scottsdale pendulum, triangle, and kimura.
- See the enriched [[GB1-W09-A3 - Armbar from Guard]] and [[GB1-W13-A3 - Kimura from Closed Guard]] for the shared GB affiliate details on arm feed and hip-driven finishes.

## Common Mistakes
- Reaching for punches instead of jamming at the source (loses frames and gets hit).
- Insufficient hip elevation before committing to the armbar leg entry (no angle = easy defense).
- Low leg placement on the finishing leg (loses the cutting angle; opponent stacks or rolls).
- Ignoring the stack defense — must use the hip escape/shuffle to re-create angle (GB Scottsdale cue).

## Key Principles
- **Jam at the source, control the committed arm**: Frames first, then trap the extended arm. The committed limb becomes your entry.
- **Hip elevation + posting hip base (GB Scottsdale)**: The same elevation and weight distribution mechanics reviewed in the affiliate Week 9/13 closed guard attacks apply directly here for posture break and stability.
- **High leg placement creates the angle**: Knee line high over the shoulder (GB affiliate triangle cue) makes the armbar tight and hard to defend against forward pressure.
- **Hip escape for retention under pressure**: When they drive or stack, the backward hip shuffle to re-create the angle (exact mechanic from GB Scottsdale triangle deep review) keeps the threat alive and often opens the triangle or kimura transition.
- **Threat chaining**: Punch defense → armbar threat → triangle/kimura/back take reactions. GB1 trains these as a connected system (Week 5 feeds Week 9/13).
- **Angle > strength**: Creating the proper angle with hips and high leg placement works against larger, stronger, or posturing opponents.

## Media & Visual References

**GB Affiliate Priority (per GB Greensboro rule):**
- Gracie Barra Scottsdale Weekly Technique Videos playlist (https://www.youtube.com/playlist?list=PLr-z5vDCpY3L0Z4aj3Sy0pgVXQjhnAUkW) — primary GB affiliate source for the connected closed guard attack system (Week 9 Pendulum, Week 13 Kimura/Triangle mechanics). Specific public long-form GB affiliate video for the exact "Week 5 block punches" title was limited after Greensboro-first searches; we therefore use the closely related GB Scottsdale content for the shared hip elevation, posting hip base, arm control, high leg placement (knee line), and hip escape for angle recovery principles that directly apply to punch defense entries and finishes. All mechanics cross-checked for GB1 curriculum alignment.
- GB Scottsdale "Pendulum Sweep from Closed Guard - Week 9" (https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=qto5bw_eEfQ) and "Kimura Attack from Closed Guard - Week 13" (https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=L3sPRsVbEa4) for the detailed hip and grip mechanics referenced above.

**Cross-references to GB-reviewed triangle retention cluster:**
- The knee line, hip escape to re-create angle under stack, posting hip weight, and arm feed details from the GB Scottsdale triangle series are the foundation for the armbar entry and retention phases in this Week 5 card.
- [[GB1-W09-A3 - Armbar from Guard]] and [[GB1-W13-A1 - Block Punches from Closed Guard + Clinch + Triangle Choke]] (both enriched with the same GB affiliate cues in the current wave).

**Recommended Photos / Diagrams**
- Strong forearm/shin frame jamming a punch in closed guard.
- Arm isolation + hip elevation with posting hip weight visible.
- High leg (knee line) entry for the armbar cut.
- Hip escape/shuffle recovering the angle under forward drive.

**Hand Placement & References**  
The progression from punch jam → high sleeve/wrist control → hip elevation with posting base → high leg armbar (knee line) is the integrated hand/hip placement. These details are taken directly from the GB Scottsdale closed guard attack reviews (triangle, kimura, pendulum) and should be drilled together with [[GB1-W09-A3 - Armbar from Guard]], [[GB1-W13-A3 - Kimura from Closed Guard]], and the Week 13 clinch + triangle card.

GB1-W05-B1 - Block Punches from Closed G.body:
## Concept

# Block Punches from Closed Guard + Distance Mgmt + Up Kick + Technical Lift

**Position:** Closed Guard  
**Category:** Sweep  
**Curriculum:** GB1 Week 5 B1  
**Confidence:** 4/5

When the opponent is postured and throwing punches in your closed guard, you block, create distance or close it strategically, use an up kick to off-balance, and execute a technical lift sweep to come to top position.

The core hip elevation, posting hip base, and angle creation mechanics are the same GB-reviewed principles from the Scottsdale affiliate closed guard attack series (already deeply reviewed for triangle retention, kimura, and pendulum).

## Setup

Opponent postured in closed guard, throwing punches or reaching.

- Frames to manage the strikes.
- Hips active for the lift and sweep.
- One leg ready for the up kick or hook.

Wikilink connections:
- Direct partner to [[GB1-W05-A1 - Block Punches from Closed Guard + Distance Mgmt + Control Arms + Armbar]] (same entry, different finish).
- Hip elevation and technical lift concepts build on [[GB1-W01-A3 - Scissor Sweep]] and connect forward to [[GB1-W09-A2 - Pendulum Sweep]] and [[GB1-W13-A2 - Sit-Up Sweep]].
- Posture breaking overlaps with the entire triangle retention cluster.

## Execution

### Manage Punches and Create Opportunity
1. Frame and jam the punches with shins or forearms.
2. As they commit or overextend, use an up kick (or knee shield) to create space or off-balance while maintaining closed guard control.

### Hip Elevation + Technical Lift
1. Drive your knees to your chest (hip elevation) while pulling with the arm control to break their posture.
2. Shift weight to the posting hip for base, then use the technical lift (bridge + hip drive) to sweep them over the top.
3. Follow to top position (side mount or mount) with control.

### Hand Placement & References
- **Punch management to grip**: Same forearm frames → sleeve/wrist or collar control as the A1 variant and the GB Scottsdale reviewed arm control in kimura/triangle entries.
- **Hip elevation with posting base**: The lift is powered by the closed guard hips. Weight on the posting hip during the drive — direct application of the GB Scottsdale pendulum and triangle "posting hip weight" cue.
- See the enriched [[GB1-W09-A2 - Pendulum Sweep]] for the shared hip timing and weight shift details.

## Common Mistakes
- Reaching instead of framing (gets hit and loses position).
- Weak hip elevation — the technical lift requires the posture break first.
- Losing the posting hip base during the drive (flattens and loses power).
- Not following through to dominant top position.

## Key Principles
- **Frame first, then attack**: Manage the immediate threat before committing to the sweep.
- **Hip elevation is the engine (GB affiliate cue)**: The same elevation mechanics reviewed in GB Scottsdale Week 9/13 content power the technical lift.
- **Posting hip for stability and power**: Weight shift to the stable hip while the active hip and lift do the work — consistent across the GB-prioritized closed guard family.
- **Up kick as setup, not the finish**: The kick creates the window; the hips and technical lift finish the reversal.
- **Chain to submissions**: If the sweep is stuffed, the same position often allows the armbar or triangle from the A1 card or Week 13 variants.

## Media & Visual References

**GB Affiliate Priority:**
- Gracie Barra Scottsdale Weekly Technique Videos playlist and specific Week 9 Pendulum / Week 13 content (see the detailed citations in the A1 card above). The hip elevation, posting hip base, and angle creation principles directly apply to the technical lift sweep after punch defense.
- Limited specific public GB affiliate long-form for the exact Week 5 B1 title after Greensboro-first searches; shared mechanics from the reviewed GB Scottsdale closed guard attack series used for fidelity to the user's training curriculum.

**Cross-references:**
- [[GB1-W09-A2 - Pendulum Sweep]] (GB Scottsdale Week 9 video) for the hip timing and weight shift details.
- [[GB1-W05-A1 - Block Punches from Closed Guard + Distance Mgmt + Control Arms + Armbar]] (sister card in this small group).

**Recommended Photos / Diagrams**
- Frame + up kick against postured opponent.
- Hip elevation with clear posting hip weight.
- Technical lift moment (bridge + hip drive).
- Arrival in side mount with control.

**Hand Placement & References**  
Punch frame → arm control → hip elevation with posting base → technical lift. The hip mechanics are taken from the GB Scottsdale reviews and should be drilled together with the other Week 5 and Week 9/13 closed guard cards in this wave.

GB1-W01-A3 - Scissor Sweep.md.body:
## Concept

# Scissor Sweep

**Position:** Closed Guard  
**Category:** Sweep  
**Curriculum:** GB1 Week 1 A3  
**Confidence:** 4/5

The scissor sweep is a foundational closed guard sweep that uses hip elevation to create an angle combined with a scissoring leg action to reverse the opponent. It is one of the first sweeps taught in GB1 and the base for the entire guard sweep family (pendulum, tripod, sit-up, etc.).

The hip elevation and angle creation mechanics are the same principles deeply reviewed from GB Scottsdale affiliate instruction for the later Week 9/13 closed guard attacks.

## Setup

Closed guard. Opponent has base, often with weight slightly biased or one knee up.

- Collar or sleeve control on the side you will sweep toward.
- Hips active and ready to elevate.

Wikilink connections:
- Direct foundation for [[GB1-W09-A2 - Pendulum Sweep]], [[GB1-W05-B2 - Tripod Sweep]], [[GB1-W13-A2 - Sit-Up Sweep]], and the Week 5 block punches sweeps.
- Hip elevation and posture concepts overlap with X-Collar and the triangle retention cluster.

## Execution

### Create the Angle with Hips
1. Use your closed guard to elevate the opponent's hips toward you while shifting your own hips to the side (the side with their weaker base or raised knee). This creates the critical angle.
2. Maintain strong sleeve or collar grip on the arm on the sweep side.

### Scissor Action + Drive
1. Swing one leg across (the scissor leg) while using the other leg to hook or push their base leg.
2. Drive through with the hips and the scissoring action to rotate them over.

### Finish to Top
1. Follow the opponent to the top, maintaining arm control.
2. Establish side mount or mount.

### Hand Placement & References
- **Primary grip**: Sleeve or collar on the sweep-side arm (same posture control feed as X-Collar four-finger and GB Scottsdale triangle entry).
- **Hip elevation + posting hip**: The sweep is powered by the hips. Weight shifts to the posting hip for base during the drive — the same detail reviewed in GB Scottsdale pendulum and triangle content.
- See the enriched [[GB1-W09-A2 - Pendulum Sweep]] (GB Scottsdale Week 9) for the evolved version of this hip timing and weight shift.

## Common Mistakes
- Insufficient hip elevation before the scissor (no angle = no sweep).
- Wrong direction or weak hook on the base leg.
- Losing the arm grip during the rotation.
- Flattening your own hips instead of staying active with posting hip weight.

## Key Principles
- **Elevation creates the angle**: Hip lift toward you is first and most important (foundational GB1 concept, reinforced by GB affiliate later-week instruction).
- **Posting hip for base and power**: Weight on the stable hip while the active hip and scissor leg drive — consistent GB Scottsdale cue across the closed guard family.
- **Grip maintains connection**: The sleeve/collar grip lets the hips do the work without the opponent posting out.
- **Foundation for the whole sweep family**: Scissor → Pendulum → Tripod/Sit-Up. The hip mechanics are the same; the leg actions evolve.
- **Threat + sweep window**: The same hip escape/angle mechanics used for triangle retention create openings for sweeps when the opponent reacts defensively.

## Media & Visual References

**GB Affiliate Priority:**
- Gracie Barra Scottsdale Weekly Technique Videos playlist (https://www.youtube.com/playlist?list=PLr-z5vDCpY3L0Z4aj3Sy0pgVXQjhnAUkW) and the specific Week 9 Pendulum video (https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=qto5bw_eEfQ). The hip elevation, posting hip weight, and angle creation principles reviewed there are the direct evolution of the foundational scissor sweep. Greensboro-first searches for specific GB affiliate scissor content were limited; we use the closely related GB Scottsdale closed guard sweep/attack series for curriculum-aligned mechanics.

**Cross-references:**
- [[GB1-W09-A2 - Pendulum Sweep]] (GB Scottsdale Week 9) for the detailed hip timing and weight shift.
- The GB Scottsdale triangle retention cues (knee line, hip escape for angle) apply when the opponent defends the scissor by driving forward.

**Recommended Photos / Diagrams**
- Hip elevation with clear angle (opponent's base compromised).
- Scissor leg action with hook leg visible.
- Posting hip weight during the drive.
- Arrival in side mount.

**Hand Placement & References**  
The sleeve/collar grip on the sweep-side arm + hip elevation with posting base is the core placement. These mechanics are the foundation for all the later GB-prioritized sweeps in this wave and should be drilled together with [[GB1-W09-A2 - Pendulum Sweep]], [[GB1-W05-B2 - Tripod Sweep]], and the triangle retention cards.

GB1-W05-B2 - Tripod Sweep.md.body:
## Concept

# Tripod Sweep

**Position:** Closed Guard  
**Category:** Sweep  
**Curriculum:** GB1 Week 5 B2  
**Confidence:** 4/5

The tripod sweep is a closed guard sweep used when the opponent defends the scissor or other entries by posting a hand or basing out. You use the opponent's post as the "third leg" of the tripod and sweep them over it with hip elevation and leg drive.

The hip elevation, posting hip base, and angle mechanics are the same GB-reviewed principles from the Scottsdale affiliate content.

## Setup

Closed guard. Opponent has defended a prior sweep attempt by posting a hand on the mat or basing strongly.

- Control the posted arm or the opposite side.
- Hips active.

Wikilink connections:
- Direct follow-up to [[GB1-W01-A3 - Scissor Sweep]] and [[GB1-W05-B3 - Outside Hook Sweep (When Opponent Defends Tripod)]] (the defensive reaction chain).
- Hip and angle concepts connect to the full sweep family and triangle retention.

## Execution

### Use the Post as the Pivot
1. As the opponent posts a hand to defend the scissor or other sweep, immediately control that posted arm or the opposite side.
2. Elevate your hips to load the sweep while shifting angle toward the posted side.

### Drive Over the Tripod
1. Use your legs to hook or push while driving with the hips to rotate them over the posted hand (the "third leg" of the tripod).
2. Follow to top.

### Hand Placement & References
- **Control the post**: Grip on the posted hand/arm or the far side to prevent them from recovering base. Same control principle as the arm isolation in the GB Scottsdale reviewed kimura and triangle entries.
- **Hip elevation + posting hip**: Same mechanics as scissor/pendulum. Weight on the stable hip during the drive.

## Common Mistakes
- Not recognizing the post early enough (miss the window).
- Insufficient hip elevation (can't drive them over the base).
- Losing control of the posted arm during the rotation.

## Key Principles
- **The post is the opportunity**: What the opponent uses to defend becomes the pivot point for your sweep.
- **Hip elevation loads the drive**: Same GB affiliate hip principle as the rest of the closed guard sweep family.
- **Posting hip stability**: Weight on the stable hip while the active hip and legs finish.
- **Chain reactions**: Defending one sweep (scissor) creates the tripod; defending the tripod creates the outside hook (B3). GB1 trains the defensive reaction tree.

## Media & Visual References

**GB Affiliate Priority:**
- Gracie Barra Scottsdale Weekly Technique Videos playlist and Week 9 Pendulum content for the shared hip elevation and posting hip base mechanics. Specific GB affiliate long-form for the exact tripod title was limited after Greensboro-first searches; we use the reviewed GB Scottsdale closed guard sweep/attack series for the core principles.

**Cross-references:**
- [[GB1-W01-A3 - Scissor Sweep]] and [[GB1-W05-B3 - Outside Hook Sweep (When Opponent Defends Tripod)]] (the reaction chain in this small group).
- GB Scottsdale pendulum and triangle reviews for the hip details.

**Recommended Photos / Diagrams**
- Opponent posting hand; you controlling it and elevating hips.
- Drive over the posted hand (tripod pivot).
- Posting hip weight visible.

**Hand Placement & References**  
Control of the posted arm + hip elevation with posting base. The hip mechanics are from the GB Scottsdale reviews and should be drilled with the scissor and pendulum cards.
